brand.full: Gevher Nesibe Healing House and Medical School
brand.tagline: 800 Years of Legacy: The World’s First Medical Faculty and Healing Center.

hero.title: A Scientific Journey Rooted in the Will of Gevher Nesibe Sultan.
hero.lead: The pinnacle of Seljuk civilization: established in Kayseri in 1206, a unique heritage combining medical education and treatment under one roof. Where music, water, and compassion turn into healing.

items.title: The First Medical Faculty
items.body: The world's first institutional example where theoretical education (Medrese) and practical application (Hospital) were conducted together.

items.title: Holistic Healing
items.body: A vision where patients were healed not only physically but also spiritually, blending music, water, and herbal medicine.

items.title: Endowment Culture
items.body: A humanitarian approach offering free service to everyone without discrimination, as per the will of Gevher Nesibe Sultan.

video.title: Watch the History Shaping the Future of Medicine

posters.title: Posters
posters.note: A special collection including architectural plans of the complex, Seljuk medical instruments, and treatment scenes.

historicalContext.title: Selçuklu Science World and STEM
historicalContext.body: The observation, recording, and measurement techniques used 800 years ago at Gevher Nesibe form the basis of today's modern medical disciplines. As part of our 'Cultural Bridge from Roots to Future' project, we re-interpret this ancient heritage through STEM disciplines (Science, Technology, Engineering, Mathematics).

carousel.title: The Will of Gevher Nesibe Sultan
carousel.description: On her deathbed, she requested her brother to build a place that would provide cure and education for all, leading to the birth of this center.

carousel.title: Corridors of Healing
carousel.description: The vaulted corridors of the Double Madrasa, a masterpiece of Seljuk architecture, designed with unique acoustics to carry water and music therapy to every room.

carousel.title: The Power of Sound and Water
carousel.description: 800 years ago, music therapy and the soothing sound of water were integrated into clinical treatments for psychological disorders.

carousel.title: The First Medical University
carousel.description: The 'Double Madrasa' layout integrated clinical practice and theoretical education under one roof, a first in medical history.

carousel.title: Cultural Bridge to the Future
carousel.description: The observation and recording tradition of the Seljuk medical world is carried to the future today through the science and technology-focused project of the Sirius Team.

historyPage.title: History & Legacy
historyPage.subtitle: The Story of Gevher Nesibe Sultan, Daughter of Kılıç Arslan
historyPage.intro: Gevher Nesibe Sultan, daughter of Seljuk Sultan Kılıç Arslan II, changed the world of medicine with her will. Upon her death, her brother Gıyaseddin Keyhüsrev I built this 'Double Medrese' to keep her name alive.
bullets: 1204-1206: Construction and commissioning of the Healing House (Hospital).
1210-1214: Completion of the Gıyasiye Medrese (Medical School).
800-Year Timeline: Serving continuously as the oldest and largest medical center in Anatolia.
Tomb of Gevher Nesibe: The eternal resting place of the Sultan located at the heart of the complex.
historyPage.task: What is the greatest similarity between the Seljuk understanding of 'Healing-Service' and modern medical ethics?
historyPage.taskHint: Hint: Consider the dignity of the patient, the right to free treatment, and the master-apprentice discipline of physicians.
historyPage.stem: Science + History of Science: The Seljuks' plant catalogs in pharmacology (pharmacy) are a product of systematic observation that inspires today's biotechnology.
historyPage.bridgeTitle: A Bridge from Yesterday to Today
historyPage.bridge: The 'Tımarhane' (Mental Health) records kept during the Seljuk period are now accepted as the first clinical case files of modern psychiatry. This tradition of data keeping is the foundation of today's big data medicine.
bibliography: Nil Sarı, 'Gevher Nesibe Sultan and Her Healing House', Turkish Archives of Medical History.
Ahmed Süheyl Ünver, 'Medicine in the Seljuk Era', Turkish Historical Society Publications.
Exhibition Data from the Kayseri Seljuk Civilization Museum (Gevher Nesibe Complex).
Osman Turan, 'Turkey in the Time of the Seljuks', Otuken Publishing.

hospitalPage.title: Architecture & Education
hospitalPage.subtitle: The Double Medrese: Where Science and Healing Intertwine
hospitalPage.intro: Architecturally, the complex consists of two main sections connected by a corridor. This structure is tangible proof of Seljuk engineering and educational vision.

sections.title: Shifahiye (Hospital)
sections.body: Located in the western part, this section contains 24 rooms. Patients were divided into different wards based on their ailments, with specialized treatments applied to each.

sections.title: Gıyasiye (Medrese)
sections.body: The medical school located in the eastern part. Students read the works of Galen, Avicenna (Ibn Sina), and Rhazes (Razi), and practiced their theoretical knowledge in the adjacent building.

sections.title: Acoustics and Light
sections.body: The layout of the structure was designed with genius acoustics to maximize sunlight and ensure the sound of water reached every room.

hospitalPage.disclaimer: This information is based on data from the Kayseri Seljuk Civilization Museum (Gevher Nesibe Complex).

casesPage.title: Healing Arts
casesPage.subtitle: Recovery through Music, Water, and Compassion
casesPage.intro: Seljuk medicine views the human not just as a body but as a whole with its soul. The following methods represent the scientific treatment techniques applied at Gevher Nesibe in the 13th century.

internationalPage.title: Scientific Heritage
internationalPage.subtitle: The Science Magnet of the Medieval World
internationalPage.intro: At the time of its establishment, Gevher Nesibe was a scientific center that hosted scholars not only from Anatolia but also from the Middle East and Europe.
steps: Meeting of Different Cultures: The fusion of Arabic, Persian, and Greek medical traditions in the Seljuk crucible.
Traveling Physicians: Doctors trained in Kayseri carried knowledge to centers like Damascus, Baghdad, and Cairo.
Translation Activities: Bringing ancient medical works into Seljuk Turkish and Arabic.
Impact on Modern Medicine: The influence of Seljuk hospital models on the establishment of Europe's medical faculties.

faqs.q: Was surgery performed at Gevher Nesibe?
faqs.a: Yes, 13th-century surgical instruments (scalpels, forceps) displayed in the museum prove that eye and internal disease surgeries were performed.

faqs.q: How long did the education take?
faqs.a: While the duration of education depended on the student's ability, it generally varied between 5-7 years within a master-apprentice relationship.

internationalPage.disclaimer: This section is for general historical information; consult medical history sources for academic research.

contactPage.title: Visit & Contact
contactPage.subtitle: See the Complex Today
contactPage.intro: The Gevher Nesibe Complex, serving today as the Seljuk Civilization Museum, awaits visitors in the heart of Kayseri.
channels: Address: Inside Mimar Sinan Park, Gevher Nesibe Neighborhood, Kayseri
Entrance: Entry with MuseumCard and official tickets.
contactPage.mapPlaceholder: Location of the complex inside Kayseri Mimar Sinan Park.
contactPage.emergency: Remember that you are in a historical site; please protect the artifacts.

virtualTourPage.title: 3D Virtual Tour
virtualTourPage.subtitle: Explore the Complex Step by Step
virtualTourPage.intro: You can explore the historical atmosphere, healing methods, and the legacy of music/water therapy of the Gevher Nesibe Healing House and Madrasa (Seljuk Civilization Museum) through the TRT Haber documentary video.

footer.disclaimer: This site was prepared for the promotion of a historical and cultural heritage. It does not contain medical advice.
footer.rights: © {year} Gevher Nesibe Legacy — Sirius Team (11/A) | Kayseri Ticaret Vocational and Technical Anatolian High School

stem.title: Scientific Heritage STEM
stem.subtitle: Cultural Bridge from Roots to Future

activity.theme: Scientific Heritage STEM - Cultural Bridge from Roots to Future
activity.purpose: This activity invites students to build a 'cultural bridge' between the scientific heritage of the past and the technological world of the future.
activity.maarif: In line with the Turkey Century Education Model; it aims to bring values education, holistic learning, interdisciplinary approach, and skill-based education into the field.

durak2.title: Station 2: History of Health and Science
durak2.description: The Gevher Nesibe Healing House is a unique center where medicine was placed on a scientific basis and the tradition of observation and recording was initiated during the Seljuk period.
durak2.mission: The healing wisdom of the past sheds light on today's modern medical technology.
durak2.maarifModel: Critical thinking and cultural awareness are the basis of our project.

details.methods: In addition to surgical operations, 'Holistic Treatment' was applied 800 years ago using music (makam), water sounds, and herbal remedies. This is a precursor to the psychiatric treatments of that period.
details.science: Seljuk medicine is built on 'observation and experiment'. Under the guidance of the works of Avicenna and Rhazes, specific diet and makam prescriptions were prepared for patients, and all symptoms were meticulously recorded.
details.connection: The foundations of today's modern psychiatric records and plant-based drug (pharmacology) technologies are a continuation of this deep-rooted Seljuk tradition of data keeping and research.

tasks: The Healing House is both a medical school and an application center.
Treatment with music and water sounds is a first in the world.
The tradition of scientific recording is the basis of modern medicine.

conclusion.title: Legacy for the Future
conclusion.body: Gevher Nesibe's legacy is not just made of stone buildings. It is a torch of science passed down through generations, etched into the collective memory of humanity. Today, as the Sirius Team, we take up this torch and bridge the ancient healing knowledge of the Seljuks with today's technology.

scienceAnalysis.title: Scientific Analysis: Depth of Treatment Methods

sections.title: 1: Biology: Humoral Theory and Homeostasis
sections.intro: This section explains the 'Humoral Theory' (Humoral Pathology), which forms the basis of the Seljuk medical understanding, from a biological and physiological perspective.
sections.explanation: Explanation: Under the heading 'Fluid Balance (Homeostasis)', human anatomical structure and four main body fluids (Humors) called 'Blood', 'Yellow Bile', 'Black Bile', and 'Phlegm' are modeled with test tubes. Balance of these fluids (Homeostasis) means health; imbalance means disease.
sections.scientificRelation: Scientific Relation (History): Seljuk physicians based disease diagnosis on physiological signs such as pulse control, urine color, and body temperature. Treatment aims to rectify this imbalance. This approach is an early form of the concept of internal body balance in modern biology.

sections.title: 2: Chemistry: Phytotherapy and Chemical Compounds
sections.intro: This section shows the pharmaceutical aspect of the healing house and the chemical analysis of herbal medicines.
sections.explanation: Explanation: Under the heading 'Analysis of Active Ingredients', medical plants such as 'Willow Bark', 'Poppy', 'Aloe Vera' and their separation in a laboratory environment (Extraction and Preparation) are visualized. For example, the 'Salicylic Acid' molecular model derived from the chemical structure of willow bark and its functions (Painkiller, Antipyretic) are shown in detail.
sections.scientificRelation: Scientific Relation (History): This proves how phytotherapy, used since ancient times and applied in the healing house, transformed into modern pharmacology (drug science). Willow bark is the basic ingredient of aspirin today.

sections.title: 3: Physics and Biophysics: Acoustics and Nervous System
sections.intro: This column examines the most unique treatment method of the healing house, sound and music, with its effects on the nervous system.
sections.explanation: Explanation: Under the heading 'Sound Waves and Nerve Modulation', the acoustic structure in Seljuk architecture and the effects of sound waves emitted from this source on the brain's nervous system are modeled. 'Acoustic Energy and Propagation' and 'Neurological Brain Waves' (Alpha and Theta waves) are shown with diagrams.
sections.scientificRelation: Scientific Relation (History): The Seljuks knew experimentally that the frequency and intensity of sound changed the human mood and designed the architecture for this purpose. This forms the basis of Music Therapy and Nerve Modulation techniques used in neurology today.

conclusion.title: Conclusion and Discussion: Linking with Today's Health Applications
conclusion.body: The methods used in the past are directly related to today's medicine and form the basis of modern health. Instead of being seen as 'primitive', they should be evaluated as the 'pioneer' and 'inspiring' stage of modern medicine.
points: Similarities (Medical Education and Holistic Approach): Gevher Nesibe is the world's first example of the system of having a hospital and medical school together (Education and Research Hospital model) applied today.

Differences (Method and Technology): Today, diagnosis is based on definitive evidence with laboratory tests and imaging technologies (MRI, CT), while the Seljuks relied on pulse and physiological symptoms.
conclusion.final: In conclusion, the Gevher Nesibe Healing House is a joint field study of natural sciences and history of science. It is a unique resource for understanding the evolution of medical history.

pulseScience.title: Biometric Data and the 'Lie Detector': The Science of Pulse
pulseScience.lead: The medicine practiced at Gevher Nesibe Darüşşifa was largely based on Avicenna's medical philosophy. In this system, the pulse was not merely a simple symptom showing heart rate; it was a biometric data set mapping the patient's entire internal system, acting as a lie detector, and initiating a personalized treatment process.

dataAnalysis.title: 1. Multi-dimensional Data Analysis (Ilm-i Nabz)
dataAnalysis.intro: While today we generally evaluate the pulse only by 'rate', physicians of that era examined the pulse across 10 different parameters (Width, Strength, Speed, Rest, Texture, Fullness, Heat, Equality, Regularity, Musical Rhythm).

points.d: The area the pulse hit the fingers was measured in 3D. They checked whether the beat was superficial or if it needed deep pressure towards the bone.
points.extra: This 3D measurement is identical in logic to how a modern ECG reads electrical signals. The physician used fingertip pressure at different depths to record data on each axis separately.

points.d: If all beats were equal, the system was healthy. If one was strong and another weak, an 'Arrhythmia' was detected.
points.extra: Regular irregularity (Nizamlı İhtilaf) meant the body was fighting illness; completely chaotic beats (Nizamsız İhtilaf) signaled critical danger. Modern medicine calls this 'arrhythmia classification'.

points.d: The highest diagnostic parameter. The ratio between 'movement' and 'rest' times was checked against musical harmony.
points.extra: According to Avicenna, a healthy child's pulse resembled Hicaz makam, a youth's resembled Rast, and an elder's resembled a slow Rehavi. When rhythm broke, the corrective makam was prescribed.

points.d: The beat suddenly stops in the middle, then continues. Accepted as a sign of heart failure.
points.extra: This mid-cycle interruption in cardiac contraction is today defined as 'intermittent heart block'. Physicians of the era interpreted it as the temporary depletion of the 'pneuma' (life spirit).

points.d: The vessel is very hard and feels like rubbing against a jagged surface. Used in diagnosing pleurisy.
points.extra: The hardness of the vessel wall (Sulb pulse) is described in Avicenna's Canon as the distinguishing diagnostic criterion for pleurisy. The physician tested this with a pressurized beat.

points.d: Beats are so weak they feel like ant footsteps. The most dangerous pulse indicating depleted life energy.
points.extra: This pulse type is specifically highlighted in the Canon as a 'harbinger of death'. It shows life energy withdrawing to the core, leaving peripheral organs unserved — similar to modern septic shock.

lieDetector.title: 2. Pulse as a 'Lie Detector' for the Soul
lieDetector.intro: In Seljuk medicine, soul and body were a whole. Physicians discovered that a thought or emotional trauma instantly changed blood flow.

points.d: The physician whispered specific words (cities, names) while holding the wrist, measuring the 'Biometric Jump' (arrhythmia) upon emotional trigger.
points.extra: Before applying this method, the physician calmed the patient to establish a 'baseline pulse'. Spikes above this baseline at emotional trigger words were recorded — exactly like a modern polygraph's GSR measurement.

points.d: For a sick young man, Avicenna listed names. A pulse spike at a specific name led him to diagnose the illness as 'love'.
points.extra: This case is one of the earliest documented psychosomatic diagnoses in medical history. After diagnosis, Avicenna facilitated the marriage and the young man quickly recovered. He recounts it himself in the Canon.

points.d: Body heat rises. Pulse becomes extremely fast, wide, and the vessel wall hard (Rebellion of Rising Heat).
points.extra: The sudden increase of bile humor interpreted as anger was classified as a temporary flare of 'Hot and Dry' temperament. Cooling makams and foods were immediately applied as treatment.

points.d: Heat and blood pull inward. Pulse suddenly weakens and goes deep; rhythm may become chaotic (Escape to the Center).
points.extra: The constriction of vessels around the brain and heart during fear is defined in modern medicine as 'sympathetic nervous system activation'. Physicians metaphorically described it as the body 'retreating to the castle'.

points.d: In sorrow, pulse is 'like a thread', slow and weak. In shame, it fluctuates inconsistently like a wave.
points.extra: Shame and grief were classified as emotions increasing 'Cold and Moist' phlegm humor. Treatment involved lively makams to balance this humor and avoidance of heavy foods.

algorithm.title: 3. Personalized Treatment Algorithm
algorithm.intro: Pulse data combined with physical traits created a personalized 'Temperament Map', which programmed the entire treatment.

points.d: Temperament (Hot, Cold, etc.) was detected via parameters like heat (speed), dryness (vessel hardness), and moisture (softness).
points.extra: Creating the temperament map was not a one-time static process. The physician visited each morning to re-measure the pulse and recalibrate the algorithm according to the current state.

points.d: If the pulse was weak/slow (Cold), upbeat makams like Uşşak or Irak were played to stimulate the patient.
points.extra: The 'Principle of Opposites' guided makam selection: hot makam for cold temperament, cold makam for hot. Modern psychoacoustic research supports this; certain frequencies genuinely affect pulse and breathing rate.

points.d: If the pulse was hard/fast (Hot), calming makams like Rast or Rehavi were chosen to relax the vessel and lower fever.
points.extra: Uşşak makam has been observed in modern animal studies to increase heart rate. Rast makam corresponds to a frequency range known for sedative effects.

points.d: For Choleric (Hot-Dry) patients, foods like vinegar, cucumber, and lettuce were prescribed to soften the sharp pulse.
points.extra: The Mutfak-ı Şerif menu changed daily; each morning's pulse reading determined that day's food 'prescription', specially prepared for the patient. This parallels today's precision nutrition approach.

points.d: For Phlegmatic (Cold-Moist) patients, honey, ginger, and black cumin were coded in the kitchen like medicine.
points.extra: The warming foods had scientific backing: ginger is known in modern medicine for anti-inflammatory and circulatory properties; honey is a natural prebiotic. Seljuk physicians discovered these effects through observational data.

points.d: The physician updated the treatment by re-measuring the pulse every morning. Doses were reduced as the pulse approached balance.
points.extra: When the pulse reached 'Itidal' (balance) at the end of the feedback loop, the patient was considered healed and intensive treatment was gradually reduced. This cycle could last weeks or months.

bibliography: Avicenna, The Canon of Medicine (El-Kanun fi't-Tıbb), Book 1 - The Section on Pulse.
Articles on Pulse in Islamic Medicine by Prof. Dr. Ayten Altıntaş and Prof. Dr. Nil Sarı.

didYouKnow.title: Did You Know?
didYouKnow.subtitle: Surprising Facts from 800 Years of Medical History
didYouKnow.intro: Discover fascinating facts about the Gevher Nesibe Healing House and the Seljuk medical world that have remained in the dusty pages of history, leaving even today's modern science in awe.

items.title: Scientific History Note
items.body: While mental patients in Europe were chained and burned saying 'the devil entered them', in Gevher Nesibe Healing House, patients were treated with humane and scientific methods using water sounds, flower scents, and musical makams.

items.title: First Integrated System
items.body: Gevher Nesibe is the world's first Education and Research Hospital model where the hospital (Healing House) and the medical school (Madrasa) were intertwined. Students would take theoretical lessons in the classroom and immediately pass through the next door to practice at the patient's bedside.

items.title: Free and Equal Healthcare
items.body: As per the last will of Princess Gevher Nesibe and the foundation charter, every patient coming to the healing house received completely free treatment and medication without any discrimination of religion, language, race, or wealth. Discharged poor patients were also given pocket money and new clothes.

items.title: Music Therapy and the Secret of Makams
items.body: Did you know that different diseases and age groups were treated with specific musical makams in the healing house? For example, Hicaz makam was played for childhood diseases, Rehavi for mental illnesses, and Uşşak makam for heart conditions.

items.title: Women's Role in Leadership
items.body: The Gevher Nesibe Healing House was established with the personal wealth and last will of a female royal, representing the largest scientific investment of the era. It stands as the most tangible monument to the strong position of women in science, endowments, and governance in Seljuk society.

pulseAnalyzerPage.title: İlm-i Nabız Analyzer
pulseAnalyzerPage.subtitle: Analyze Your Biometric Data with Avicenna's Healing Algorithm
pulseAnalyzerPage.intro: Analyze your pulse by entering your heart rate (BPM) or using the interactive rhythm adjuster below. Discover your temperament and emotional state according to Avicenna's 10-dimensional Science of Pulse.
pulseAnalyzerPage.caution: Note: This analysis is based on historical medical texts and does not carry modern medical diagnostic value.
pulseAnalyzerPage.settingsIntro: You can customize the pulse ranges (BPM) below where the Avicenna analyzer triggers specific emotional states.

fear.name: Fear and Terror (Dehşetli)
fear.desc: Pulse is slow and deep. Life energy and blood have pulled to the center, cooling the peripheral organs.
fear.gida: Ginger, honey, black cumin, cinnamon (warming foods)

sadness.name: Sorrow and Grief (Kederli)
sadness.desc: Pulse is thread-like, slow, and weak. The body slows down and sorrow envelopes the soul.
sadness.gida: Fresh broth, honey, almonds, figs (moist and hot foods)

balance.name: Itidal (Balanced / Healthy)
balance.desc: Pulse is moderate in strength, rhythmic, and regular. Represents the perfect state of internal balance (homeostasis).
balance.makam: Rast or Nihavend (to maintain the current balance)
balance.gida: Seasonal vegetables, light grains, pure water (diet free of excess)

anger.name: Anger and Fury (Öfkeli)
anger.desc: Pulse is fast, hard, vessel wall is tense, and beat area is wide. Bile and heat in the body are elevated.
anger.gida: Vinegar, sour grape juice, cucumber, lettuce, cold water (cooling 'muberrid' foods)

excitement.name: Extreme Excitement / Love / Arrhythmia
excitement.desc: Pulse is extremely fast, wavy, irregular, and sometimes broken (Zü'l-fetr). Indicates mental or emotional over-stimulation.
excitement.gida: Willow bark tea, lemon water, light yogurt-based foods
